quoteFirst: M., 19th Regiment, aged seventeen, of sanguine temperament, healthy frame, was brought up to camp
quoteLast: Sept. 9th, 1855. He had been wounded in the assault upon the Redan in the upper part of the left thigh, and had been lying by the side of the ditch where he fell thirteen hours. When discovered, he was carried carefully in a soldier’s greatcoat as far as the opening of the trenches, and thence on a stretcher to camp. He was very cold and prostrate on his arrival.

quoteFirst: He said he had, and that these sounds were just like those of a battle.  An interval of quiet then ensued, when again,
quoteLast: , arose other similar explosions, but I still remained in doubt whether the enemy was engaged in blowing up his own magazines, or whether General Slocum had not felt forward, and become engaged in a real battle.

quoteFirst: Luckily for us the wind was in our favour and the fire was carried swiftly along the river-bank to the westward. I turned into my cot and slept soundly until
quoteLast: , when I woke to find that the wind had changed and that the fire was rapidly coming down upon us. The camalote immediately to windward of us had already been burnt up, but the thin wiry grass which covered the Pine Ridge and grew to the height of one's knees was as dry as tinder, and we could see in the distance some of the pine-trees catching fire and blazing up as the long line of flames swept past them.
title:
A Glimpse at Guatemala
And Some Notes on the Ancient Monuments of Central America
author: Maudslay, Anne Cary, -1926; Maudslay, Alfred Percival, 1850-1931

quoteFirst: It was about half an hour before daylight, probably
quoteLast: , in the midst of a violent storm of wind and rain, that, under cover of darkness, the Americans began crossing the river.  They were seen by the militia sentinel on guard at Queenston, who immediately ran to the guardhouse to give the alarm.
title: General Brock
